title: Kickoff Brief — intraclass: Modern Intraclass Correlation Coefficients in R
text: Build R package operationalizes generalizability-theory (GT) framework intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs) developed ten Hove, Jorgensen & van der Ark, using modern variance-component estimation rather classical ANOVA / McGraw–Wong mean-squares approach. package exist (differentiators): - Existing tools split two camps. classical ones (psych, irr, irrNA, irrICC, ICCDesign) ANOVA / mean-squares based. assume balanced data, though tolerate missing cells — irrICC (Gwet) irrNA compute ICCs wide rating matrix NAs, closest prior art incomplete designs, still within classical model rather mixed-model / MC-CI approach. model-based ones (performance::icc, gtheory, misty) extract variance-partition coefficient fitted model implement full interrater-reliability ICC family (absolute agreement vs. consistency × single vs. average × fixed vs. random raters), error-variance framing, selection framework. - GT framework’s reference code loose supplementary scripts, engineered package. - gap fill: coherent, well-tested package (1) fits variance components modern engines, (2) computes correct ICC stated design proper interval estimation, (3) handles imbalanced / incomplete / multilevel designs, (4) teaches user ICC choose . Audience: applied researchers psychology, education, medicine, behavioral science need defensible IRR estimates guidance. first-class goal, afterthought: package pkgdown site place learn ICC best practices. Every estimator’s docs every vignette must explain estimand, assumptions, tradeoffs behind “knob,” guide reader sensible choice. Estimation engines (dependency discipline): - Default engine: lme4 /glmmTMB (MLE random-effects models). framework’s 2025 simulation work favors MLE-RE Monte-Carlo CIs feasibility accuracy, spine. - Optional engines (Suggests, never Imports): brms / rstanarm (Bayesian) lavaan / blavaan (SEM / common-factor). Keep base install light fast; gate optional-engine code behind rlang::check_installed().

what: How many raters do I need? A D-study
title: Advanced: imbalanced & multilevel designs
text: ICC(*,1) reliability single rater ICC(*,k) reliability mean k raters actually used. decision (D-) study asks forward-looking question: reliable mean number raters m ? generalizability theory absolute-agreement ICC dependability coefficient, projecting m raters just change averaging divisor, Φ(m)=σs2σs2+(σr2+σres2)/m,\Phi(m) = \frac{\sigma^2_s}{\sigma^2_s + (\sigma^2_r + \sigma^2_{res}) / m}, d_study() reuses fit already — refitting. Reliability climbs raters diminishing returns, projection anchored observed: m = 4 (number raters ratings) Φ(m) exactly ICC(,k) get icc() directly. one-value can also ask icc() inline numeric unit, e.g. unit = c("single", "average", 6). Read curve, classic “many raters?” picture — plot autoplot() (needs ggplot2):  Projection extrapolation. rater variance σr2\sigma^2_r estimated many raters observed, projecting far beyond design leans hard estimate. Monte-Carlo interval widens honestly reflect rather pretending precision lacks — projecting absolute agreement refused fixed raters, wider rater universe generalize (use raters = "random").
code: library(intraclass)  fit <- icc(ratings, score, subject, rater, seed = 1) proj <- d_study(fit, m = 1:8) proj #> # D-study projection: two-way random, absolute agreement #> Observed raters: 4 | CI: 95% montecarlo (10000 draws) #>       m  estimate   95% CI #>       1     0.290   [0.050, 0.706] #>       2     0.449   [0.096, 0.828] #>       3     0.550   [0.137, 0.878] #>       4     0.620   [0.175, 0.906] #>       5     0.671   [0.210, 0.923] #>       6     0.710   [0.241, 0.935] #>       7     0.741   [0.271, 0.944] #>       8     0.765   [0.298, 0.950] library(ggplot2) autoplot(d_study(fit, m = 1:12))

what: Multilevel ICCs: subject vs. cluster level
title: Advanced: imbalanced & multilevel designs
text: Everything treats subject object measurement. subjects often nested higher-level clusters — pupils classrooms, patients clinics — “reliability” splits two: Subject level (within-cluster): reliably raters distinguish subjects within cluster? Cluster level (-cluster): reliably raters distinguish cluster means? Ignoring nesting conflates biases (ten Hove, Jorgensen & van der Ark, 2022). Passing cluster column icc() fits multilevel model reports level separately. Consider pupils nested classrooms, pupil rated panel raters. simulate design substantial classroom-level signal modest within-classroom differences: levels come back one call. cluster-level ICC higher two: raters agree classrooms score high pupils within classroom — exactly pattern expect true variation lives classrooms. number report depends decision make: classroom-level intervention cares cluster-level reliability, pupil-level one subject level. Request just one level = "subject" level = "cluster".
code: library(intraclass)  set.seed(2025) n_class <- 16 n_pupil <- 5 n_rater <- 4 grid <- expand.grid(   pupil = seq_len(n_pupil),   classroom = seq_len(n_class),   rater = seq_len(n_rater) ) class_effect <- rnorm(n_class, sd = 1.3)[grid$classroom] pupil_effect <- rnorm(n_class * n_pupil, sd = 0.6)[   (grid$classroom - 1) * n_pupil + grid$pupil ] rater_effect <- rnorm(n_rater, sd = 0.4)[grid$rater] school <- data.frame(   classroom = factor(grid$classroom),   pupil = factor(paste(grid$classroom, grid$pupil, sep = "_")),   rater = factor(grid$rater),   score = 10 + class_effect + pupil_effect + rater_effect +     rnorm(nrow(grid), sd = 0.7) ) icc(school, score, subject = pupil, rater = rater, cluster = classroom, seed = 1) #> ℹ Treating raters with the same label in different clusters as the same raters #>   (crossed with clusters, Design 1). #> ℹ If each cluster has its own raters, give them cluster-unique labels or pass #>   `design = "nested_in_clusters"`. #> # Intraclass correlation: multilevel two-way random, absolute agreement #> Subjects: 80 in 16 clusters | Raters: 4 (random) | Observations: 320 (complete) #> Engine: glmmTMB (REML) | CI: 95% montecarlo (10000 draws) #>   level    index     estimate   95% CI #>   subject  ICC(A,1)    0.431   [0.251, 0.561] #>   subject  ICC(A,k)    0.751   [0.573, 0.836] #>   cluster  ICC(A,1)    0.880   [0.000, 0.972] #>   cluster  ICC(A,k)    0.967   [0.000, 0.993] #> Variance components: cluster 0.998, subject 0.461, rater 0.136, cluster:rater 0.000, residual 0.473 #>  #> This message is displayed once per session.

title: Modern Intraclass Correlation Coefficients
text: intraclass computes interrater-reliability intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs) within generalizability-theory framework, using modern variance-component estimation (linear mixed models) rather classical ANOVA / mean-squares approach. aims (1) fit variance components modern engines, (2) compute correct ICC stated design proper (boundary-aware Monte-Carlo) interval estimation, (3) handle imbalanced, incomplete, multilevel designs, (4) help decide ICC choose, — docs website place learn ICC best practice, just call functions. [!NOTE] package approaching first release. full interrater-reliability ICC family implemented — two-way (absolute agreement vs. consistency, single vs. average, random vs. fixed raters) one-way designs, imbalanced incomplete (missing-cell) data, multilevel designs (subject vs. cluster level, raters crossed nested clusters/subjects) — boundary-aware Monte-Carlo intervals. Fits run mixed-model engines (glmmTMB, lme4) SEM engine (lavaan). Choosing ICC decision guide, choose_icc() helper, D-study projection rater counts (d_study()), autoplot() plots shipped. Bayesian engine roadmap; see project/MILESTONES.md.

code: library(intraclass)  icc(ratings, score, subject, rater, seed = 2024) #> # Intraclass correlation: two-way random, absolute agreement #> Subjects: 6 | Raters: 4 (random) | Observations: 24 of 24 cells (complete) #> Engine: glmmTMB (REML) | CI: 95% montecarlo (10000 draws) #>   index     estimate   95% CI #>   ICC(A,1)    0.290   [0.053, 0.715] #>   ICC(A,k)    0.620   [0.182, 0.910] #> Variance components: subject 2.556, rater 5.244, residual 1.019 #> Shrout & Fleiss equivalent: ICC(A,1) = ICC(2,1), ICC(A,k) = ICC(2,k) choose_icc(model = "twoway", type = "consistency", unit = "average", raters = "random") #> # Recommended ICC #> Design: two-way random, consistency #> Recommendation: ICC(C,k) #> Why: #>   - Crossed (two-way): the same raters judge every subject. #>   - Consistency: only the rank order must match; a constant per-rater offset is forgiven. #>   - Average: you will act on the mean of your raters. #>   - Random raters: a sample you generalize beyond, to the rater universe they were drawn from. #> Run this on your data: #>   icc(data, score, subject, rater, type = "consistency", unit = "average") #> Notes: #>   - Complete vs. incomplete is automatic: icc() uses whatever ratings are present and projects ICC(*,k) to the effective number of ratings (k_eff). The design must stay connected, or icc() fails loudly. set.seed(1) grid <- expand.grid(pupil = 1:5, classroom = 1:12, rater = 1:4) grid$score <- with(grid,   10 + rnorm(12, sd = 1.2)[classroom] +     rnorm(60, sd = 0.6)[(classroom - 1) * 5 + pupil] +     rnorm(4, sd = 0.4)[rater] + rnorm(nrow(grid), sd = 0.7)) school <- data.frame(   pupil = factor(paste(grid$classroom, grid$pupil, sep = "_")),   classroom = factor(grid$classroom),   rater = factor(grid$rater),   score = grid$score)  icc(school, score, subject = pupil, rater = rater, cluster = classroom, seed = 2024) #> # Intraclass correlation: multilevel two-way random, absolute agreement #> Subjects: 60 in 12 clusters | Raters: 4 (random) | Observations: 240 (complete) #> Engine: glmmTMB (REML) | CI: 95% montecarlo (10000 draws) #>   level    index     estimate   95% CI #>   subject  ICC(A,1)    0.322   [0.162, 0.484] #>   subject  ICC(A,k)    0.655   [0.436, 0.790] #>   cluster  ICC(A,1)    0.870   [0.005, 0.973] #>   cluster  ICC(A,k)    0.964   [0.018, 0.993] #> Variance components: cluster 1.036, subject 0.305, rater 0.150, cluster:rater 0.005, residual 0.492

title: Recommend an ICC and the call that computes it — choose_icc
code: # Two-way absolute agreement, single rater, random raters (Shrout & Fleiss # ICC(2,1)): the two structural defaults (crossed, non-multilevel) are implied. choose_icc(type = "agreement", unit = "single", raters = "random") #> # Recommended ICC #> Design: two-way random, absolute agreement #> Recommendation: ICC(A,1) #> Shrout & Fleiss equivalent: ICC(A,1) = ICC(2,1) #> Why: #>   - Crossed (two-way): the same raters judge every subject. #>   - Absolute agreement: the value itself must match; a systematic difference between raters counts as error. #>   - Single rater: you will act on one rater's score. #>   - Random raters: a sample you generalize beyond, to the rater universe they were drawn from. #> Run this on your data: #>   icc(data, score, subject, rater, unit = "single") #> Notes: #>   - Complete vs. incomplete is automatic: icc() uses whatever ratings are present and projects ICC(*,k) to the effective number of ratings (k_eff). The design must stay connected, or icc() fails loudly.  # Consistency of the average of fixed raters -- McGraw & Wong ICC(C,k): choose_icc(type = "consistency", unit = "average", raters = "fixed") #> # Recommended ICC #> Design: two-way mixed, consistency #> Recommendation: ICC(C,k) #> Shrout & Fleiss equivalent: ICC(C,k) = ICC(3,k) #> Why: #>   - Crossed (two-way): the same raters judge every subject. #>   - Consistency: only the rank order must match; a constant per-rater offset is forgiven. #>   - Average: you will act on the mean of your raters. #>   - Fixed raters: exactly these judges; the coefficient does not generalize past them. #> Run this on your data: #>   icc(data, score, subject, rater, type = "consistency", raters = "fixed", unit = "average") #> Notes: #>   - Random raters is the recommended default for interrater reliability; use fixed only when these are the entire population of raters you will ever use. #>   - Complete vs. incomplete is automatic: icc() uses whatever ratings are present and projects ICC(*,k) to the effective number of ratings (k_eff). The design must stay connected, or icc() fails loudly.  # A one-way design (interchangeable raters): type/raters do not apply. choose_icc(model = "oneway", unit = "single") #> # Recommended ICC #> Design: one-way random #> Recommendation: ICC(1) #> Shrout & Fleiss equivalent: ICC(1) = ICC(1,1) #> Why: #>   - One-way: raters are interchangeable across subjects, so systematic rater differences are absorbed into error -- the most conservative ICC. #>   - Single rater: you will act on one rater's score. #> Run this on your data: #>   icc(data, score, subject, rater, model = "oneway", unit = "single") #> Notes: #>   - Complete vs. incomplete is automatic: icc() uses whatever ratings are present and projects ICC(*,k) to the effective number of ratings (k_eff). The design must stay connected, or icc() fails loudly.  # A multilevel design, both levels: choose_icc(type = "agreement", unit = "single", raters = "random",   multilevel = TRUE, level = "both") #> # Recommended ICC #> Design: multilevel, two-way random, absolute agreement #> Recommendation: #>   subject: ICC(A,1) #>   cluster: ICC(A,1) #> Why: #>   - Crossed (two-way): the same raters judge every subject. #>   - Absolute agreement: the value itself must match; a systematic difference between raters counts as error. #>   - Single rater: you will act on one rater's score. #>   - Random raters: a sample you generalize beyond, to the rater universe they were drawn from. #>   - Both levels: within-cluster (subject) and between-cluster (cluster) reliability side by side. #> Run this on your data: #>   icc(data, score, subject, rater, cluster, unit = "single") #> Notes: #>   - Complete vs. incomplete is automatic: icc() uses whatever ratings are present and projects ICC(*,k) to the effective number of ratings (k_eff). The design must stay connected, or icc() fails loudly. #>   - See vignette("advanced") for a worked multilevel example.

what: Which ICC is this, and when should you use it?
title: Intraclass correlation coefficient for a two-way design — autoplot.icc
text: Three choices pin coefficient: Agreement vs. consistency (type). Absolute agreement treats systematic differences raters (rater main effect, \(\sigma^2_r\)) error: use actual value matters raters must agree number (clinical scores, measurements). Consistency ignores constant per-rater offset: use relative standing matters. large gap two signals big systematic differences rater level – rating-procedure problem worth fixing. Single vs. average (unit). ICC(*,1) reliability single rater; ICC(*,k) reliability mean k raters. Report ICC(*,k) averaged score use. Random vs. fixed raters (raters). Random treats raters sample wish generalize beyond – recommended default interrater reliability. Fixed treats raters interest forgoes generalization; fit separately (raters fixed effects), balanced data matches random point estimate incomplete data genuinely differs. icc() warns choose . Fixed-rater consistency classic Shrout & Fleiss ICC(3,1).

title: Intraclass correlation coefficient for a two-way design — autoplot.icc
text: single rating per subject--rater cell, subject--rater interaction pure error separately identified; sum, residual variance \(\sigma^2_{res}\), estimable. Absolute agreement counts rater main effect \(\sigma^2_r\) error; consistency drops : $$ICC(,1) = \sigma^2_s / (\sigma^2_s + \sigma^2_r + \sigma^2_{res})$$ $$ICC(,k) = \sigma^2_s / (\sigma^2_s + (\sigma^2_r + \sigma^2_{res}) / k)$$ $$ICC(C,1) = \sigma^2_s / (\sigma^2_s + \sigma^2_{res})$$ $$ICC(C,k) = \sigma^2_s / (\sigma^2_s + \sigma^2_{res} / k)$$ \(\sigma^2_s\) subject (signal) variance k number raters.

title: Intraclass correlation coefficient for a two-way design — autoplot.icc
text: subjects nested higher-level clusters (pupils classrooms, patients clinics), single-level ICCs conflate levels biased (ten Hove et al. 2022). Supplying cluster fits five-component Design-1 model $$score \sim 1 + (1|cluster) + (1|cluster{:}subject) + (1|rater) + (1|cluster{:}rater)$$ reports two distinct reliabilities. subject level (within-cluster) asks reliably raters distinguish subjects within cluster: signal -subject-within-cluster variance cluster variance drops . cluster level (-cluster) asks reliably raters distinguish cluster means: signal -cluster variance rater-disagreement error cluster--rater term. Choose level matches decision make (subject, cluster). agreement/consistency single/average choices apply level. design inferred data (ten Hove et al. 2022, Table 2). raters crossed clusters (rater rates every cluster) five-component model used (Design 1). design read rater labels, rater label appears one cluster taken rater (crossed). raters cluster-specific share labels (e.g. "rater 1"/"rater 2" reused every cluster – nested design), give cluster-unique labels declare design = "nested_in_clusters"; otherwise design treated crossed icc() prints one-time note assumption. raters nested clusters (cluster raters; Design 2) four-component model fit, rater variance carried nested rater-within-cluster term. raters nested subjects (subject raters; Design 3) rater variance confounded residual, giving three-component multilevel one-way model reports agreement-ICC(1)/ICC(k). nested designs define subject level – cluster-level ICC needs raters crossed clusters – level restricted "subject" . Mixed patterns (raters crossed, nested) supported design raise error. crossed design (Design 1) additionally supports incomplete data – subjects rated different, overlapping rater subsets (missing cells) – computing subject-level ICCs REML averaging divisor set effective number ratings per subject (k_eff, harmonic mean), exactly single-level incomplete two-way ICC . Identifiability checked first: cluster's subject--rater layout must connected, absolute agreement raters must bridge clusters (otherwise design really rater-nested). missing cells make crossed-vs-nested pattern ambiguous, declare design (). incomplete data cluster level reported single-rater ICC(c,1) (raters bridge clusters); averaged cluster-level ICC(c,k) incomplete data yet supported (effective number raters per cluster still validated). averaged unit requested cluster level incomplete data, row dropped (message) rather failing whole call, subject-level averages single-rater cluster ICC still returned. Fixed raters (raters = "fixed") supported crossed design subject level balanced, complete data: rater main effect becomes finite-population variance observed raters (McGraw & Wong Case 3A), consistency identical random-rater case absolute agreement differs term. Incomplete nested designs, incomplete nested fixed-rater designs, fixed-rater cluster level remain later milestones. Nested designs still require balanced, complete data.

title: intraclass: Modern Intraclass Correlation Coefficients — intraclass-package
text: Estimates interrater-reliability intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs) within generalizability-theory framework using modern variance-component estimation (linear mixed models) rather classical analysis--variance mean-squares approach. Provides full ICC family (absolute agreement versus consistency, single versus average, fixed versus random raters, one-way versus two-way) boundary-aware Monte-Carlo confidence intervals, support imbalanced, incomplete, multilevel (nested) designs, decision-study projection numbers raters, interactive helper choosing correct coefficient. Multilevel methods follow ten Hove, Jorgensen van der Ark (2022) doi:10.1037/met0000391 .

title: Rater reliability example (Shrout & Fleiss, 1979) — ratings
text: six-target, four-judge worked example Shrout Fleiss (1979), long, one-rating-per-row format icc() consumes. Every subject rated every rater (complete, balanced two-way design), reference case icc() returns canonical coefficients ICC(,1) = 0.290, ICC(,k) = 0.620, ICC(C,1) = 0.715, ICC(C,k) = 0.909.

title: Rater reliability example (Shrout & Fleiss, 1979) — ratings
code: icc(ratings, score, subject, rater, seed = 2024) #> # Intraclass correlation: two-way random, absolute agreement #> Subjects: 6 | Raters: 4 (random) | Observations: 24 of 24 cells (complete) #> Engine: glmmTMB (REML) | CI: 95% montecarlo (10000 draws) #>   index     estimate   95% CI #>   ICC(A,1)    0.290   [0.050, 0.713] #>   ICC(A,k)    0.620   [0.173, 0.909] #> Variance components: subject 2.556, rater 5.244, residual 1.019 #> Shrout & Fleiss equivalent: ICC(A,1) = ICC(2,1), ICC(A,k) = ICC(2,k)

title: Rater reliability example with missing cells — ratings_incomplete
code: summary(icc(ratings_incomplete, score, subject, rater, seed = 2024)) #> # Intraclass correlation: two-way random, absolute agreement #> Subjects: 6 | Raters: 4 (random) | Observations: 20 of 24 cells (incomplete) #> Engine: glmmTMB (REML) | CI: 95% montecarlo (10000 draws) #>   index     estimate   95% CI #>   ICC(A,1)    0.249   [0.038, 0.693] #>   ICC(A,k)    0.521   [0.114, 0.881] #> ICC(*,k) projects to an effective 3.27 raters (harmonic mean of ratings/subject). #> Variance components: subject 2.281, rater 5.532, residual 1.344 #> Shrout & Fleiss equivalent: ICC(A,1) = ICC(2,1), ICC(A,k) = ICC(2,k) #> Absolute agreement counts the rater main effect (systematic differences in rater level) as error. #> A single rating per cell confounds the subject-by-rater interaction with #> residual error.

title: intraclass 0.1.0
text: First public release. intraclass estimates interrater-reliability intraclass correlation coefficients (ICCs) within generalizability-theory framework using modern mixed-model variance-component estimation (rather classical ANOVA mean-squares approach), boundary-aware Monte-Carlo confidence intervals guidance choosing right coefficient. Every estimator verified independent oracles — published worked examples, psych/ANOVA cross-checks, alternate engines, seeded simulations.

title: intraclass 0.1.0
text: icc() computes full interrater-reliability ICC family linear mixed model: absolute agreement vs. consistency (type), single vs. average (unit), random vs. fixed raters (raters), one-way vs. two-way (model) designs — classic Shrout & Fleiss / McGraw & Wong coefficients — reported boundary-aware Monte-Carlo confidence interval Shrout & Fleiss equivalent. second interval method, ci_method = "bootstrap": parametric bootstrap simulates response vectors fitted model, refits, takes percentile quantiles (boot_samples resamples). rely asymptotic-normal covariance approximation Monte-Carlo default uses, cost refit per resample. Available every design "glmmTMB" (simulate() + refit) "lme4" (bootMer) engines fit — two-way random fixed, one-way, multilevel designs — levels. fixed raters finite-population  recomputed directly refit. Imbalanced incomplete (missing-cell) designs handled directly mixed model: uses effective number ratings k_eff (harmonic mean per-subject counts) ICC(*,k) divisor aborts loudly disconnected, unidentified design. Multilevel ICCs subjects nested clusters — pupils classrooms, patients clinics — following ten Hove, Jorgensen & van der Ark (2022). Supply cluster column get subject-level (within-cluster) cluster-level (-cluster) coefficients via level. Covers raters crossed clusters (Design 1) nested clusters/subjects (Designs 2–3), complete incomplete crossed data, fixed raters subject level.

title: intraclass 0.1.0
text: Default glmmTMB engine (boundary-robust REML), selectable engine = "lme4" (via merDeriv) covers every design glmmTMB — two-way random fixed raters, one-way, multilevel designs (crossed nested) levels — balanced incomplete/ragged data, agreeing glmmTMB point estimate Monte-Carlo interval (ragged fit lands variance-component boundary falls back glmmTMB). selectable engine = "lavaan" — SEM common-factor generalizability model (Jorgensen 2021) whose absolute-agreement coefficient uses indicator-mean rater-variance estimator — covers random two-way design. Optional engines live Suggests, base install stays light.

title: intraclass 0.1.0
text: choose_icc() — interactive programmatic decision helper recommends ICC report, explains choice, emits exact icc() call run. gives advice ; fit. d_study() — projects fitted ICC’s reliability mean arbitrary number raters (generalizability decision study), autoplot() reliability curve; icc()’s unit also accepts numbers one-projections. autoplot() / plot() methods icc objects draw coefficient forest plot variance-component decomposition; tidy() / glance() give tidy summaries. Plotting needs ggplot2 (Suggests dependency).

text: d_study() now projects one-way fit (Spearman-Brown projection ICC(1)) instead erroring. fixed-rater multilevel call (icc(..., cluster =, raters = "fixed")) now works default level, dropping deferred cluster level subject level (nested-design path already ) rather requiring explicit level = "subject". incomplete crossed multilevel data, requesting averaged cluster-level ICC(c,k) now drops just (unsupported) row message returns subject-level single-rater cluster results, instead failing whole call. incomplete crossed multilevel design every subject rated now reported unidentified rather returning spurious ICC = 0.5. icc() prints one-time note multilevel design inferred crossed shared rater labels, nested design reused, cluster-relative labels silently treated crossed. mc_samples seed validated clear, classed errors; invalid values (e.g. mc_samples = 0/1, fractional non-numeric value) longer produce silent NA interval bare base-R error. degenerate fit variance component now fails loudly instead returning NaN estimate, unstable fit whose Monte-Carlo draws overflow reported rather silently truncated.
